Title: Keys on Doormats: Exposed API Credentials on the Web

URL Source: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.12498v2

Published Time: Tue, 17 Mar 2026 02:35:14 GMT

Number of Pages: 18

Markdown Content:
# Keys on Doormats: Exposed API Credentials on the Web 

Nurullah Demir 

Stanford University nurullah@cs.stanford.edu 

Yash Vekaria 

UC Davis yvekaria@ucdavis.edu 

Georgios Smaragdakis 

TU Delft and Stanford University g.smaragdakis@tudelft.nl 

Zakir Durumeric 

Stanford University zakir@cs.stanford.edu 

Abstract —Application programming interfaces (APIs) have become a central part of the modern IT environment, allowing developers to enrich the functionality of applications and inter-act with third parties such as cloud and payment providers. This interaction often occurs through authentication mechanisms that rely on sensitive credentials such as API keys and tokens that require secure handling. Exposure of these credentials can pose significant consequences to organizations, as malicious attackers can gain access to related services. Previous studies have shown exposure of these sensitive credentials in different environments such as cloud platforms and GitHub. However, the web remains unexplored. In this paper, we study exposure of credentials on the web by analyzing 10M webpages. Our findings reveal that API credentials are widely and publicly exposed on the web, including highly popular and critical webpages such as those of global banks and firmware developers. We identify 1,748 distinct credentials from 14 service providers (e.g., cloud and payment providers) across nearly 10,000 webpages. Our findings reveal that most exposures are introduced during dynamic bundling, deployment, or resource inclusions rather than their occurrence in static website code that prior works have primarily focused on. Moreover, our analysis of archived data suggest credentials to remain exposed for periods ranging from a month to several years. We characterize web-specific exposure vectors and root causes, finding that most originate from JavaScript environments—especially bundled files and third-party inclusions—highlighting unique issues in the software supply chain. We also discuss the outcomes of our responsible disclosure efforts that demonstrated a substantial reduction in credential exposure on the web. We conclude with best practices for the web community to mitigate and protect against exposure of credentials. 

1. Introduction 

Developers rely on application programming interface (API) keys to authenticate their applications to third-party services (e.g., cloud platforms, payment processors). These credentials serve as access tokens that identify and authorize the application or developer making API requests [54]. Despite security guidelines advising against hard-coding secrets [25], [41], [45], it is common for developers to embed keys in source code or configuration files for convenience, enabling their code to connect to required services without interactive logins or more complex secret storage mecha-nisms [48]. However, once code containing credentials is shared (especially in public resources), those secrets become exposed posing potential for misuse. Several high-profile incidents highlight the risks associ-ated with exposed credentials. In one notorious case, Uber’s 2016 data breach was traced to attackers finding an Amazon Web Services (AWS) access key embedded in code posted on GitHub [21], [57]. The attackers used that credential to access Uber’s cloud infrastructure, exposing data of 57 million users. Similar exposures have affected major organizations, such as Slack and The New York Times, where API credentials were exposed in public resources, leading to unauthorized access or data compromise [1], [55]. These incidents demonstrate that even a single exposed credential can enable large-scale abuse, data theft, or manipulation of infrastructure. Beyond direct financial abuse, such as unauthorized resource consumption, exposed credentials can compromise customer privacy, facilitate large-scale data exfiltration, and damage the provider’s reputation. Studies show this rapid exploitation timeline and show that many credentials persist online for days or weeks [40], [61]. The industry’s response is to conduct frequent codebase audits for exposed credentials. Given the prevalence of cre-dential exposures, practitioners have developed detection tools such as TruffleHog [56] and Gitleaks [63] to detect exposed se-crets that implement extensive regex-based detectors for API keys, tokens, and use entropy-based analysis to detect high-entropy strings that likely correspond to sensitive data such as credentials private keys [19]. Major platforms have integrated automated scanning to mitigate accidental exposure. GitHub’s 

secret scanning [24] feature identifies over 800 secret types and alerts maintainers when exposures occur. GitLab [26] and Bitbucket [4] have adopted similar measures. Cloud providers now react directly to such detections: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) automatically disables public service account keys [34], and Microsoft Azure issues immediate notifications to account owners when keys appear in public repositories [7]. These initiatives reflect a broader recognition among service providers that exposed credentials pose multifaceted risks. Overall, credential exposures remain a systemic challenge for modern software ecosystems. As applications become increasingly interconnected, the number of credentials in circulation grows, amplifying the risks of exposure. However, 

> arXiv:2603.12498v2 [cs.CR] 16 Mar 2026

the extent of credential exposure on the web has not been systematically examined yet, despite “Security Misconfigu-ration” recently becoming the second most significant risk on the web [43]. In this paper, we address this gap through a large-scale dynamic analysis of 10 million domains, identifying exposed credentials on the web, examining their persistence and remediation through responsible disclosures, and an-alyzing the technical and organizational factors contribut-ing to their exposure. We find that credential exposure on the web is a widespread malpractice spanning diverse sectors—including critical infrastructure such as global banks and healthcare—and posing potentially significant real-world risks to millions of users worldwide. Our contributions can be summarized as follows: 

• Web-scale analysis of credential exposure. We conduct the first large-scale dynamic analysis of API credential exposure on the web, analyzing over ten million websites. We show that dynamic analysis is vital for finding these exposures; while prior work heavily focuses on analyzing static repositories or source code, we find that the majority of web-based exposures occur only in live production envi-ronments. Through this approach, we identify 1,748 verified credential exposures from 14 critical service types. 

• Root causes and recommendations. We characterize web-specific exposure vectors, finding that 84% of verified exposures manifest within JavaScript resources —specifically inside bundled files (62%) — while 16% propagate through third-party inclusions. Based on empirical evidence and disclosure feedback, we identify the underlying technical and organizational causes, such as insecure build pipelines and environment variable misconfigurations, and provide actionable, web-specific recommendations for stakeholders. 

• Persistence and disclosure outcomes. With our longi-tudinal analysis, we show that exposed credentials often persist for months to years—averaging 12 months of public accessibility—highlighting a severe, long-term security risk. Furthermore, our large-scale responsible disclosure effort led to the removal of about half of the exposed credentials across analyzed services within two weeks. 

• Supply-chain and systemic risks. We demonstrate that these exposures act as direct vectors for critical supply-chain and systemic compromise. For instance, we uncovered exposed credentials granting access to cloud infrastructure of a “Global Systemically Important Financial Institution (GSIB)” [5], as well as full repository privileges of a devel-oper responsible for maintaining firmware used in devices such as drones worldwide, demonstrating severe real-world risks that extend far beyond the web ecosystem. 

2. Background 

In this section, we provide the terminology and the threat model relevant to our study. 

Terminology. We define terminology used throughout this paper. A hostname refers to a fully qualified host identifier, including subdomains (e.g., foo.bar.com ). A domain refers to the eTLD+1, i.e., the registrable portion of a hostname (e.g., 

bar.com ). We use the term website to denote all hostnames that share the same eTLD+1 domain (e.g., both bar.com 

and foo.bar.com are considered part of the same website). A webpage denotes a specific URL path or resource (e.g., 

https://www.bar.com/test.html ). These definitions are applied consistently throughout the study. 

Threat Model. We model a passive adversary that has no privileged access to web servers but can collect and analyze publicly retrievable web content through ordinary browsing or automated crawling. Such an adversary can index, store, and inspect any client-visible resource, including JavaScript files, API responses, or embedded configuration data, to discover exposed credentials. The attacker cannot compromise servers, intercept encrypted traffic, or obtain secrets through insider or malware access. Once a credential becomes part of a publicly reachable resource, however, it is effectively acces-sible and verifiable through the corresponding service API. Our measurement therefore represents a worst-case exposure scenario, quantifying the prevalence and origin of client-side credential leakage rather than simulating exploitation. 

3. Related Work 

Credential Exposure. Prior research on credential exposure has primarily looked at non-web ecosystems—comprising of studies focused on platforms such as GitHub [20], [22], [40], [42], [60], Cloud [8], mobile apps [2], [66], VS code extensions [35], InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) [62], Docker images [15], and mini-programs [65]. More recently, El Yadmani et al. [19] conducted a comprehensive analysis of secret leaks in misconfigured cloud buckets by large-scale scanning of public cloud storage. Other works have adopted qualitative methods to understand real-world developer prac-tices, such as socio-technical causes of accidental credential exposures [29] and password-choice habits [36]. Collectively, these studies reveal that credential exposure occurs across diverse ecosystems. Additionally, a recent report by Truffle Security explored credential exposure on the web using static CommonCrawl archives [31]. However, by relying only on static WARC files, their methodology captures only the initial HTTP response [13], limiting their focus to a vector that our data indicates accounts for only 5% of total web-specific exposures. Consequently, this static approach misses the vast majority of dynamically introduced exposures. Furthermore, this analysis does not distinguish between intentionally public, safe-to-expose keys and genuinely sensitive private credentials, which may lead to an overestimation of actionable vulnerabilities. 

Vulnerability Disclosure Studies. In web security com-munity, researchers have studied responsible disclosures to address security vulnerabilities such as unintended abuse of server infrastructure [11], [58], HTTPS misconfigura-tions [64], and many others [32], [49], [53]. Over the years, the literature has systematically studied the effectiveness of different notification strategies, including communication channels [32], [37], [52], message content [38], [39], [64], 10M HAR Files     

> use TruffleHog
> to extract secrets
> Extracted
> Credentials Database
> Selecting
> critical s ervices
> Selected Services Credential
> Validation
> Credential Exposure Measurement
> Responsible Disclosures
> BigQuery
> Detected
> Exposures Third-party
> Disclosure
> Notifications
> First-party
> security@
> abuse@
> webmaster@
> info@
> Daily Crawl to Monitor Remediations Data
> Analysis
> Extracting
> emails security.txt @

Figure 1: Overview of our measurement pipeline to detect and validate credential exposures on the web (top), followed by responsible disclosure and remediation monitoring (bottom). sender identity [11], [39], recipient characteristics [9], [33], and interdependencies among these factors [10], [59]. Our research is not a vulnerability notification study to understand effectiveness of different notification controls. It is rather, the first large-scale measurement study of credential exposure on the web. To this end, we take inspiration from the literature on vulnerability disclosures to devise our responsible disclosure notifications by adopting best practices recommended in these works. 

How does our work differ from prior research? We advance prior work by providing new dimensions to the study of credential exposure: (i) rather than analyzing static artifacts (e.g., source code repositories) that miss production-only exposures, we conduct a large-scale dynamic analysis of the rendered web, demonstrating that the vast majority of credential exposures manifest exclusively in live production environments—introduced dynamically during build and deployment workflows; (ii) beyond detection, we quantify how long credentials remain exposed and assess real-world remediation through our responsible disclosure efforts; and (iii) grounded in empirical evidence and disclosure feedback, we identify the technical and organizational root causes of these web-native exposures and highlight their broader im-plications, including real-world supply-chain risks affecting critical infrastructure. 

4. Methodology 

In this section, we describe our methodology for detecting exposed credentials on public web resources (in Section 4.1), analyzing their persistence (in Section 4.2), and conducting responsible disclosure (in Section 4.3). 

4.1. Identifying Exposed Credentials 

We begin by outlining our methodology for identifying exposed credentials on public web resources and verifying their validity, summarized in Figure 1. 

Data Collection. To analyze whether credentials are ex-posed through public web resources, we rely on the dataset provided by HTTPArchive, which collects web performance and network data since 2010 from the public web [28].It uses the Chrome User Experience Report (CrUX) [27], which represents the most visited websites by real Chrome users. HTTP Archive’s dataset includes network requests, responses, cookies, and headers for all resources loaded by each webpage, including JavaScript files, stylesheets, and other assets. This data is accessible through Google BigQuery. In addition, the complete crawl data is available in the HAR 

(HTTP Archive) format—a JSON-based representation that includes the raw request and response data for all resources fetched during each crawl—and is stored in Google Cloud Storage. This format provides complete traces of every visited webpage, allowing us to analyze all resources loaded during the rendering process. For this study, we use the September 2025 crawl dataset of HTTP Archive. Each crawl in HTTP Archive typically contains two pages per origin: the landing page and an inner page (e.g., http://www.foo.com/bar.html ). Due to its significant size, overhead and higher redundancy, we restrict our analysis to the landing pages only. After filtering, our dataset consists of 11.9 million hostnames (subdomain.example.com ) and 9.3 million domains ( exam-ple.com ), representing approximately 200 TB of data. 

Identifying Exposed Credentials. Many service providers often use unique and recognizable schemes for their creden-tials (e.g., the sk_live prefix used by Stripe API creden-tials). These consistent patterns make credentials explicitly identifiable through pattern-based techniques such as regular expressions. Several open-source and commercial tools exist for detecting such exposures. In our study, we employ the open-source software TruffleHog (v3.90.8) [56], which can identify over 800 distinct secret types. We selected TruffleHog 

for its ability to detect not only single-component API keys but also compound credentials, such as key pairs. This capa-bility is particularly important for subsequent validation and contextual analysis. For instance, AWS credentials typically consist of two parts: an Access Key ID (e.g., beginning with AKIA ) and a corresponding Secret Access Key . The presence of both components enables reliable validation to distinguish real credentials from false positives. TruffleHog automatically identifies such key pairs and outputs structured metadata, which we later use for programmatic validation. 

Exposure Localization. To systematically identify where credentials appear within web resources, we designed an automated exposure localization pipeline . The pipeline oper-ates over the raw HTTP Archive (HAR) files. Each iteration retrieves a batch of HAR files directly from HTTP Archive’s Google Cloud Storage, decompresses them, and process them for analysis. In the first stage, the pipeline runs TruffleHog 

in an offline mode across all extracted HAR files without validation. TruffleHog scans each HAR file and emits detected credential patterns, including those appearing within request URLs, response bodies, JavaScript payloads, or embedded resources. As an additional step, the pipeline attempts to decode common encodings (for example, URL-encoding and Base64) in request and response fields so that credentials hidden in encoded content are revealed for analysis. In a subsequent step, our pipeline processes each detection and traverses the corresponding HAR file to determine the precise location and context of exposure. For every identified credential, we record whether it appears in the request URL, response body, or other components of a webpage. This contextual mapping provides a detailed view of where and how the credential becomes exposed. 

ò Lower-Bound Scope of Results. Overall, we iden-tified 557 distinct secret types across the analyzed web resources. Given the high false-positive rate inherent in pattern-based detection, we restrict our analysis to a subset of credentials that can be safely validated through official service APIs. As a result, all findings presented in this paper constitute an explicit lower bound on the true prevalence of exposed credentials, reflecting only verified cases. The actual security impact is likely considerably higher than what is captured in this conservative study. 

Exposure Validation. After the initial offline detection phase, our analysis revealed a diverse range of credential types embedded across web resources. From these, we selected a subset of high-impact secrets for further validation, prioritizing credentials that could potentially grant access to cloud services, developer platforms, or other sensitive infrastructure. Table 2 lists the specific secret types in-cluded in this stage. The selection of services for validation was based on the criticality and potential impact of the corresponding credentials. We prioritized providers whose keys grant (1) control over core infrastructure and cloud resources (e.g., AWS, Cloudflare, Alibaba), (2) direct financial or transactional access (e.g., Stripe, Razorpay), (3) integration with development or supply-chain systems (e.g., GitHub, Bitly), and (4) access to communication or data-delivery platforms that could be leveraged for large-scale abuse or TABLE 1: Services and used endpoints to verify credentials.                

> Service Verification endpoint(s)
> Alibaba https://ecs.aliyuncs.com (DescribeRegions) Azure https://login.microsoftonline.com/%s/oauth2/v2.0/token AWS https://sts.amazonaws.com (GetCallerIdentity) Bitly https://api-ssl.bitly.com/v4/user Cloudflare https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/user/tokens/verify https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/user GitHub https://api.github.com/user Mailchimp https://{dc}.api.mailchimp.com/3.0/ OpenAI https://api.openai.com/v1/me Razorpay https://api.razorpay.com/v1/items?count=1 SendGrid https://api.sendgrid.com/v3/scopes Slack https://slack.com/api/auth.test Stripe https://api.stripe.com/v1/charges Telegram https://api.telegram.org/bot{TOKEN}/getMe Twilio https://verify.twilio.com/v2/Services

privacy violations (e.g., SendGrid, Slack). To verify the authenticity of these credentials, our pipeline integrates a dedicated validation component that operates within a controlled and privacy-preserving environment. The validation process builds on TruffleHog ’s built-in verification functionality, which checks whether a credential is active using predefined, non-destructive API calls. These checks require authentication but do not expose, modify, or log sensitive data. For example, when validating Amazon Web Services (AWS) credentials, the pipeline calls the GetCallerI-dentity endpoint, which simply returns the account identifier associated with the key. The validation relies solely on the HTTP status code to determine authenticity, without inspecting or storing response data. Before enabling validation, we manually reviewed the corresponding validator implementations within TruffleHog to ensure that all API interactions were safe, non-destructive, and compliant with responsible disclosure practices [46]. During validation, the pipeline re-runs TruffleHog in verification mode, activating only the relevant detectors. A credential is marked as valid once the corresponding service confirms its authenticity through a successful verification response (e.g., status code 200). We never read or store any data from endpoints in any phase of this study. 

Data Enrichment. We use additional datasets to enrich our analysis. (1) We use the HTTP Archive dataset to identify the technologies utilized on the webpages which relies on Wappalyzer [3] to detect both client- and server-side software. (2) We use Cisco Umbrella’s [12] domain categorization service to classify webpages based on their content and security-related categories. (3) We use the Tranco [30] toplist to determine the popularity rank of the analyzed webpages. (4) To infer the country association of each domain, we use the CrUX dataset [27], which provides aggregated data segmented by the geographic location of real Chrome users. 4.2. Longitudinal Analysis 

To assess the persistence of exposed credentials over time, we perform a longitudinal analysis using historical web data from HTTP Archive’s BigQuery dataset. We aim to determine how long a credential remains publicly accessible across different web crawls and whether it reappears in subsequent or prior versions of the same websites. For each validated credential, we extract the corresponding website (e.g., www.foo.com ) and a truncated substring of the secret (the first 15 characters, such as AKIASECRET12345 for AWS) to avoid handling full sensitive values while maintaining sufficient entropy for reliable matching. Using this substring, we locate and re-visit the same webpages in historical HTTP Archive crawls spanning up to five years prior to our main September 2025 dataset. This enables us to identify instances where the same credentials appeared earlier (indicating prolonged exposure). Each detected reappearance is timestamped using the crawl date, allowing us to estimate exposure duration and persistence intervals. 

4.3. Responsible Disclosure 

To responsibly disclose the detected vulnerabilities to affected parties and measure their remediation over time, we adopt a systematic approach, as summarized in Figure 1. 

Identifying Disclosure Recipients. Email is a commonly used notification channel in responsible vulnerability disclo-sure literature [10], [11], [17], [19], [51]. As a result, we leverage email as our notification channel to perform respon-sible disclosures. To identify email addresses of notification recipients, we use (1) security.txt files (wherever adopted by the vulnerable website) and (2) generic email addresses as used in prior vulnerability disclosure research [50], [52] to reliably reach responsible person at the organization. The 

security.txt standard [18] as defined in RFC 9116 [23] allows a website to provide a machine-parsable file to help organizations describe their vulnerability disclosure practices, making it easier for researchers to report vulnerabilities. We identify security.txt file of a website by looking at the root of a website’s domain (i.e., /security.txt ) as well as at the well-known path (i.e., /.well-known/security.txt ). Alternatively, as defined in RFC 2142 [14], generic email addresses are also considered as standardized email addresses to contact an organization. As a result, we select security and administrator oriented email addresses to notify the vulnerable websites comprising of security@ , abuse@ , and 

webmaster@ . Additionally, we also include info@ in order to further reach out to the organization’s front office. Contacting the organization at these email addresses ensured that our notification directly reaches the relevant owner or administra-tor of the website that can understand the resultant security risk from the reported credential exposure, respond to us and timely mitigate the issue effectively. In total we notified 2,435 distinct vulnerable entities (2,279 webpages as a first-party and 156 third-parties) using 9,746 email addresses. 

Content of Disclosure Email. Figure 9 in Appendix shows the template of a sample disclosure notification. As proof of exposure—we include service name, partial credential prefix, URL associated with the exposure, and webpage on which the exposure was observed—for each credential. Use of partial credentials was to avoid further exposure of the actual credentials (in case the email is opened by someone unauthorized). Moreover, we suggest two remediation approaches: (1) removal and (2) revocation of the credentials to address the exposure. We also request feedback from the notification recipients regarding (1) cause of the exposure and (2) their awareness about the same. After one week from sending the initial emails, we follow-up with a reminder. 

Monitoring Credential Removal and Revocation. To understand the effect of our disclosure notifications and the organization’s security practices related to mitigating the reported issue(s), we monitor remediations—in terms of removal and revocation of exposed credentials—post disclosure at daily granularity as done in prior works [9], [32], [39], [52], [64]. To this end, we build a crawler (see Appendix D for technical details) that visits the set of websites with exposures and collects the network traffic data (i.e., network requests, responses, redirects, headers, and payload), and re-scans the collected data to identify any prevailing credential exposures (Section 4.1). This data allows us to measure credential removals from webpages reported in our disclosures. Additionally, we also perform a day-to-day verification of the exposed credentials detected at the beginning to measure revocation of exposed credentials. This webpage scanning and the subsequent credential verification is repeated on a daily basis, starting from the day we send our first disclosure email (that we refer to as “Day 0”), allowing us to analyze the organization’s approach towards addressing the reported exposure. 

5. Results on Exposed Credentials 

In this section, we present our results on identified credentials discovered across the web and analyze their char-acteristics. Table 2 summarizes both verified and unverified credential exposures across the analyzed services. Overall, we identify a large number of potential leaks, although only a fraction could be confirmed as verified. We further examine this discrepancy and its underlying causes in the following. 

Unverified Credentials. The observed discrepancy between verified and unverified credentials reflects the inherent lim-itations of automated detection tools such as TruffleHog ,whose broad pattern-matching approach favors high cov-erage but can also capture non-credential text fragments. Although this issue has been noted in prior work [6], we provide an exploratory analysis of it here. Our analysis shows that many service-specific detection patterns – often implemented as regular expressions – are overly generic and frequently match unrelated text segments. For example, we observed numerous false positives originating from Base64-encoded data or from function and parameter names (e.g., 

QuickOrderLineQuantityView ), which clearly do not rep-resent actual credentials. In other cases, credentials appeared TABLE 2: Distribution of Verified Credential Exposures by Service Type.                                                                                                               

> Services # Identified Credentials # Verified Credentials Verified Share # Domains # Websites # Resources # Top Affected Domain Rank Cloud Services
> AWS 1,515 283 19% 961 4,693 931 Top 500 Azure 377 73 19% 85 92 102 Top 500 Alibaba 569 29 5% 33 42 55 Top 10k Cloudflare 45,046 20 <0.1% 26 28 34 Top 50k
> Payment Services
> Stripe 654 277 42% 283 322 350 Top 1k RazorPay 1,768 134 8% 155 166 172 Top 10k
> Messaging Services
> Telegram 219 186 85% 250 257 304 Top 500 Mailchimp 492 124 25% 132 142 144 Top 10k SendGrid 255 100 40% 2,784 2,898 3,480 Top 500 Twilio 295 99 34% 68 116 113 Top 100k Slack 130 66 51% 114 120 93 Top 5k
> Developer Tools
> OpenAI 1,037 181 18% 242 256 252 Top 5k GitHub 1.9M 119 <0.1% 228 272 301 Top 500 Bitly 207 57 28% 332 400 336 Top 5k
> Total 2M 1,748 0.09% 5,693 9,804 6,667 –

syntactically correct but were likely revoked prior to our val-idation, leading to unsuccessful verification attempts. While unverified detections dominate numerically, they primarily reflect the trade-off of pattern-based detection methods, which emphasize coverage over precision, rather than genuine credential exposures. The true-positive rate, however, varies substantially across services and credential types: for instance, GitHub tokens exhibit a true-positive rate of only 0.0001%, whereas Telegram tokens reach approximately 85%. In the following, we investigate this disparity by examining 

TruffleHog ’s detection logic for these two services. The two services reveal fundamentally different detection behaviors. For GitHub, the pattern used by TruffleHog is intentionally broad to accommodate the many token types the platform issues—such as personal access tokens, OAuth keys, and fine-grained access tokens. This rule searches for any string starting with GitHub’s common prefixes (e.g., ghp_ ,

gho_ ) followed by a long sequence of letters and numbers. While this approach allows the detector to recognize a wide range of potential credentials, it also matches other long, random strings that are not credentials, for instance, parts of URLs, hashed values, or build artifacts. As a result, the GitHub detector produces many matches that look plausible but do not correspond to valid credentials, explaining the very low true-positive rate observed in our dataset. In contrast, the pattern used for Telegram tokens is narrow and unambiguous. Telegram’s pattern follow a fixed structure consisting of a numeric identifier, a colon, and an alphanumeric key (e.g., 

123456789:ABCDefGhIjKlm ) which allows the detector to identify tokens with a higher precision. Thus, our exploratory analysis indicates that the high false-positive rate stems not only from TruffleHog ’s generic detection patterns but also the heterogeneity of credential formats themselves. 

5.1. Overview of Verified Exposed Credentials 

Next, we focus on credentials that were successfully verified, indicating that they correspond to active and au-thentic keys. These verified cases represent genuine security exposures rather than merely potential leaks. In total, we identify 1,748 verified credentials across 9,804 websites and 5,693 distinct domains. Exposure is widespread across service categories, with cloud services (e.g., AWS, Cloudflare) and payment services (e.g., Stripe, Razorpay) accounting for the majority of verified credentials. AWS credentials alone represent more than 16% of all verified exposures and were found on over 4,693 websites. Email and communication services such as SendGrid and Twilio also appear frequently, with a significant portion of their exposures originating from embedded third-party resources. Moreover, credential expo-sures occur not only within first-party code but also within external dependencies, underscoring that risks extend beyond local development practices. Several verified credentials were found on highly popular domains (e.g., within the top 500 websites in the Tranco ranking), showing that even well-maintained and widely used web services are not immune to inadvertent credential exposure. In the remainder of this paper, we focus explicitly on verified credentials, as they represent confirmed and actionable security exposures that allow us to reason about the real-world impact. 

5.2. Ecosystem Analysis of Credential Exposure 

In this section, in order to understand how credentials become exposed and propagated across the web, we distin-Top 1K

> Number of Credentials

Figure 2: Distinct credential exposures over website-popularity ranks, partitioned into first-party sources (left) and third-party sources (right). First-party exposures are common at all ranks. Third-party exposures are frequent, particularly on popular third-party integrations, and often result from first-party misconfigurations. guish between first-party resources—those directly hosted by websites—and third-party resources that are externally embedded or loaded. Figure 2 shows the distribution of exposed credentials across website popularity for both first- and third-party sources. Exposures associated with first-party resources occur predominantly on less popular websites, whereas those linked to third-party resources are more frequently observed also among higher-ranked websites. This indicates structural dif-ferences in how credentials become exposed. We investigate this phenomenon in the following. 

Exposures by First-Party Resources. Next, we present our results on exposures within first-party resources, focusing on credentials that appear directly in website-owned content rather than externally embedded components. We find that 84% of distinct credentials are present in first-party resources. Thus, our results indicate that credential exposure on the web primarily originates in first-party environments, underscoring the central role of website operators in this ecosystem. Surprisingly, we find that 478 (5%) cases where credentials are hard-coded in the Document Object Model (DOM), indicating that credentials are directly embedded in the rendered page content. Our findings show notable differences across services in where credentials are exposed—whether through first-party or third-party resources. The majority of distinct credentials for GitHub (97%), Cloudflare (95%), and Bitly (95%) appear within first-party resources, suggesting that these credentials are mostly embedded directly into webpage’s code or con-figuration. As shown in Figure 2, the number of credential exposures increases as website popularity declines. At the same time, we also identify first-party exposures among highly ranked websites, indicating that credential management issues persist even among popular domains. 

Exposures by Third-Party Resources. We now discuss our results on exposures in third-party resources. Our results show 16% of exposed credentials stem from third-party resources. Among all services, OpenAI credentials exhibit the highest share of third-party exposures, with 24% of distinct OpenAI credentials appearing in externally loaded resources. Similarly, Twilio (24%), Mailchimp (23%), Alibaba (23%), Stripe (22%), and SendGrid (22%) also show notable third-party exposure rates. The majority of these services support user-facing features that might be intended for client-side use, and their appearance in web resources may therefore be intentional, even if unsafe. We further address this phe-nomenon in our disclosure analysis (in Section 6.2), drawing on feedback received from affected parties. We further analyze which third-party origins are most frequently associated with credential exposures. We find that a likr.tw accounts for the largest number of exposure occur-rences with 3,752 cases, followed by bestfreecdn.com ( 2,633 ), 

googletagmanager.com (283), and cloudfront.net (137). When considering distinct credentials, the domains googleapis.com, googletagmanager.com, amazonaws.com, and cloudfront.net 

appear most frequently, with 21, 20, 11, and 11 unique cre-dentials respectively. This discrepancy reflects differences in the responsibility for exposure. In the case of likr.tw , a single credential is reused across multiple websites through a shared script, meaning that the third party is directly responsible for the widespread exposure. In contrast, domains hosting more distinct credentials such as googletagmanager.com are 

unlikely to be the original source of exposure; rather, they reflect first-party misconfigurations where credentials are em-bedded within assets deployed. Our results indicate that while third-party domains often act as secondary distribution points for credentials originating from site-managed resources, they still play a substantial role in the overall exposure landscape. TABLE 3: Top technologies and categories on the web (HTTP Archive, HA) vs. websites with exposed credentials.                                            

> Technologies Software Categories HA (name) HA (%) Exposures (name) Exp. (%) HA (name) HA (%) Exposures (name) Exp. (%)
> jQuery 71% Google Analytics 70% JS Libraries 88% Miscellaneous 88% Open Graph 66% Open Graph 70% Miscellaneous 85% JS Libraries 83% Google Analytics 53% core-js 69% Analytics 63% JS Frameworks 76% PHP 53% jQuery 65% Programming Lang. 63% Analytics 74% Google Font API 44% Cloudflare 59% Web Servers 59% CDN 69%

5.3. Technical Exposure Environment 

In this section, we further analyze the technical environ-ment of exposed credentials to understand the underlying causes of these exposures. 

File Types. We next examine the types of files in which credentials are exposed to better understand their technical context. The majority of exposures occur within JavaScript resources (84%), followed by HTML (8%) and JSON (7%) files. Interestingly, we also identify isolated cases of creden-tials appearing in unconventional locations – for instance, a verified GitHub access token embedded in a CSS file. Since the majority of exposed credentials appear in JavaScript resources, we further investigate the underlying causes within this environment. Our exploratory analysis indicates that a large share of credentials are embedded within bundled JavaScript files. Bundling, a common optimization technique that combines multiple scripts into a single resource to improve performance [47]. To examine this systematically, we identify bundled files using runtime fingerprints inserted by popular build tools, based on the fingerprint list provided by prior work [47]. For example, bundles produced by Webpack contain the marker __webpack_require__ . Our analysis shows that 62% of credential exposures in JavaScript files occur within bundles created by popular build tools such as Webpack. It is worth noting the results are expected be higher since our analysis does not coverage all the bundlers such as custom bundlers. These findings underscore the critical necessity of dy-namic analysis. Given that the majority of credential leaks are introduced dynamically—such as during the build and deployment process, materializing within these compiled bundles—the traditional methodology of scanning static source code repositories used by prior work would miss them. Ultimately, our analysis demonstrates that live production environments represent the primary channel for web-based exposure, indicating that analyzing the rendered webpage is essential. 

Used Software. Next, we analyze the software components detected on both server- and client-side across the analyzed websites. We find an average of 17 software components per website in the broader HTTP Archive dataset, compared to 22 on websites exposing credentials (interquartile ranges 14–24 vs. 17–27). This indicates that websites with exposed credentials tend to employ a broader mix of technologies, reflecting higher integration density and aligning with ob-servations that greater software diversity often correlates with increased configuration risk [16]. Table 3 compares the most prevalent software and software categories in the HTTP Archive baseline with those observed on pages containing exposed credentials. We observe notable differences between the two groups. Websites with exposed credentials show a stronger reliance on JavaScript-based frameworks and client-side libraries, whereas widely used foundational technologies dominate the web. These findings are consistent with our earlier observations on files types, showing that a large portion of exposures originate within the JavaScript environment. 

Credential Encoding Formats. Next, we examine how credentials are represented within web resources to assess their encoding or representation form. Our framework sup-ports three encoding types for a given credential: P LAIN ,ESCAPED _U NICODE (e.g., \u006b\u0065\u0079 for key ), and B ASE 64 (e.g., a2V5 ). Most exposed credentials appear without substantial encoding, with 67% detected as P LAIN ,18% as E SCAPED _U NICODE , and 15% as B ASE 64. In prac-tice, this means that the majority of exposed credentials are human-readable, while the rest offer only cosmetic protection and can be easily reversed, and provide no real protection. 

Geographical Distribution. Next, we analyze the geograph-ical distribution of distinct exposed credentials to assess how exposure varies across countries using the CrUX’ dataset [27]. Table 4 compares each country’s share of all web domains in the HTTP Archive dataset with its share of domains containing exposed credentials in our measurements. While the overall distribution aligns with global web share, several countries show a markedly higher representation among domains with exposures. The United States, for instance, contributes 20% of all exposed domains compared to 9% of globally active domains, and India accounts for 16% of exposed domains versus 5% of global domains. 

Categories of Domains. To contextualize the affected do-mains, we classify them by their primary category. As shown in Table 5 in Appendix, business, e-commerce, and technology platforms rank among the top categories, with business services representing the largest share. Figure 3 illustrates the relationship between these website categories and the most frequently exposed credential types. The distribution indicates that each category relies on a distinct combination of external providers rather than a single dominant service. For instance, business-oriented websites show strong associations with infrastructure providers such as AWS and service platforms TABLE 4: Top 5 countries by relative share (%) of globally active web domains and of exposed websites identified in our dataset.                    

> Global Domain Share Exposed Domains Country Share (%) Country Share (%)
> United States 9.1 United States 20.0 India 4.7 India 15.7 Indonesia 4.4 Italy 5.6 Brazil 3.5 Brazil 4.3 Germany 3.5 United Kingdom 3.8

like OpenAI. Domains in the Computers and Internet cate-gory are most commonly linked to GitHub. SaaS and B2B platforms, in turn, maintain close ties with communication and payment providers such as Twilio and Stripe, consistent with their use of third-party APIs for customer interaction and transaction management. Overall, these results show that credential exposures are not uniformly distributed but instead follow the specific dependency patterns of each domain category. 

5.4. Case Studies 

To illustrate the real-world implications of credential exposure, we present two representative cases. While our study identified leaked credentials across a broad range of critical organizations—including governmental, healthcare, educational, and financial institutions—these two examples highlight the potential significant impact on critical infras-tructure and the software supply chain. 

5.4.1. Critical Infrastructure Exposure. We discovered AWS credentials belonging to one of “Global Systemically Important Financial Institutions” (G-SIFIs). Such institutions are designated by financial regulators as critical to the stability of the global financial system, as their disruption could propagate risks across markets and other interconnected entities [5]. The exposed credentials granted access to multi-ple AWS services, including Lambda, Relational Database Service, Secrets Manager, and Key Management Service. A manual investigation of the affected resources revealed additional exposed credentials and sensitive data, such as RSA private keys, OAuth2 credentials, internal service URLs, and development-related email addresses. Following our disclosure, the bank acknowledged the issue and promptly removed the credentials from the exposed resources. 

5.4.2. Supply-Chain Risk. Next, we illustrate how exposed credentials in the web can lead to concrete supply-chain risks. We identified GitHub credentials belonging to a firmware developer responsible for a firmware for a radio-transmitter which used by numerous manufacturers of drones, toys, and other remote-controlled devices globally. The exposed credentials granted full repository privileges for firmware distribution. An adversary obtaining these credentials could modify source code or firmware binaries, inject malicious 

Figure 3: Relation between five major website categories (left) and eight common third-party services (right); link widths reflect the number of distinct exposed credentials. functionality, or tamper with release artifacts distributed to downstream manufacturers. Such a compromise would prop-agate through legitimate update channels and affect physical devices globally, effectively transforming a single credential leak into a large-scale supply-chain threat. We contacted the developer, who confirmed awareness of the issue and initially stated that the credentials were limited to read access for public repositories. However, upon verification, they acknowledged that the credentials had full access. The developer promptly revoked the credentials, reviewed the commit history, and confirmed that no signs of malicious activity had been identified. 

5.5. Exposure Persistence 

In this section, we analyze how long verified credentials remain publicly accessible once exposed. To estimate their persistence, we examine nine distinct crawl snapshots from the HTTP Archive dataset, tracing when each credential first appeared and whether it continued to be accessible over time. We limit our analysis to nine measurement points due to the substantial financial overhead of large-scale historical queries, which required processing more than 1.5 PB of data. Figure 5 summarizes our findings, showing the number of verified credentials that persisted across different measurement points. At a high level, the results indicate that many exposures remain publicly accessible for extended periods – often several years – highlighting a long-term significant security risk for affected websites. On average, exposed credentials remain publicly acces-sible for 12 months (min: 1; SD: 14; max: 60). In the first month after exposure, we observe a roughly 10% reduction in the number of active credentials—suggesting that only a small fraction of affected providers detect and remove their exposed credentials promptly, while the vast majority remain accessible for months or even years. Figure 4 shows the box plots for the lifetime of exposed credentials across service types. Services such as Slack and Bitly exhibit substantially longer exposure periods, whereas OpenAI 

> AWS
> Cloudflare
> Azure
> Github
> SendGrid
> telegrambot
> Alibaba
> Stripe
> Mailchimp
> Slack
> RazorPay
> Twilio
> Bitly
> 0
> 10
> 20
> 30
> 40
> 50
> 60
> Lifetime of Exposures (Months)

Figure 4: Persistence duration of exposed credentials by service (in months). 1 Month 

> 2 Months
> 3 Months
> 6 Months
> 1 Year
> 2 Years
> 3 Years
> 4 Years
> 5 Years
> Exposure Age
> 0
> 500
> 1000
> 1500
> Verified Credentials
> 9
> 10
> 11
> 12
> 13
> 14
> Distinct Services

Figure 5: Persistence trend of exposed credentials and affected services over time. cloud providers like AWS and Azure tend to have shorter-lived exposures. OpenAI’s API also shows relatively short exposure durations; since these services were introduced late 2022 [44], the observed lifetimes for its exposures are inherently limited by the measurement window. Bitly is a notable outlier whose credentials persist significantly longer than those of any other provider. The Kruskal–Wallis H-test confirms that exposure life-times differ significantly between services ( p < 0.001 ), indi-cating that some providers’ credentials persist considerably longer once exposed. The underlying causes of these differ-ences might stem from several factors. The short observed lifetime does not necessarily imply that websites stop expos-ing credentials; it may instead indicate revocation or rotation by developers. Some services may deploy automated systems that detect and revoke exposed credentials more quickly than others, and developers may also intervene manually once they become aware of a exposure. Providing a precise explanation of these lifetime differences would require a detailed, service-specific analysis of revocation workflows and policies, which we leave for future work. 

6. Outcomes of Responsible Disclosures 

We engage in responsible disclosing the exposed creden-tials to the affected parties—2,279 first party and 156 third-party notifications—amounting to a total of 9,746 disclosure emails. In this section, we study the effect of our responsible disclosures in improving overall web security. 

Delivery Success of Disclosure Emails. Out of 9,746 emails, we observed a bounce rate of 58% (i.e., 5,658 emails), which is lower than the failure rate observed with emails in prior vulnerability notification studies [10], [11], [50], [52]. Out of different email variations attempted for each website, we were able to successfully deliver atleast one email to 1,668 websites (i.e., 68.5% success rate). Among the failed 767 websites, we adopted a manual approach for obtaining email addresses of critical infrastructure websites— financial institutions, health and medicine, and government and law—comprising 61/68 websites where we were able to find at least one email to send disclosures to. Hence, we were able to send responsible disclosures to 71% (i.e., 1,729 domains). As described in our disclosure methodology (Section 4.3), exposures on first-parties led to notifications to first-parties, whereas exposures originating from embedded third-party led to notifications to the affected third-party, with first parties additionally notified only when they were directly responsible for the integration (e.g., googletagmanager.com ). This explains the difference between the number of domains we notified and the total number of affected domains reported in Table 2. Two domains requested to opt-out and hence were removed from our analysis. In rest of this section, we focus our analysis by studying effect of our disclosures on the 1,727 websites in mitigating reported exposures. 

6.1. Impact of Disclosures on Credential Exposures 

Effect of Disclosures Across Services. To understand the effect of our responsible disclosures, we study two primary security measures taken by the notified entities to remediate against the exposure: (1) removal, and (2) revocation of the reported credentials. We use the data from our daily crawls (Section 4.3) to measure the presence or absence of pre-disclosure credentials on affected websites. Subsequently, we also perform daily verification of these credentials to understand their revocations (Section 4.1). Figure 6 depicts the impact of our disclosures on daily counts of exposed credentials from each service across affected websites in (a) and active credentials in (b). Day 0 represents the day on which we send disclosure emails and Day 0 values capture the pre-disclosure exposure counts. We can observe a clear decline in credential removals across all services with the total number of exposures reducing from 1,405 (Day 0) to 706 (Day 14), a removal of 50% of credentials by websites within the first 14 days of our disclosures. Moreover, among different services, OpenAI observes the largest percentage drop in exposures ranging from 153 on Day 1 to 44 by Day 14, a reduction by 71%, followed by Stripe (62% removals) and GitHub (57% removals); while Bitly records the lowest Day 0

(b) Effect of Credential Revocations 

Figure 6: Distribution of remediation effects from our responsible disclosures—(a) credential removals (b) and credential revocations. (a) depicts total number of exposed credentials found on web each day and (b) shows the daily counts of verified exposed credentials. Day 0 represents the measurement performed before sending the disclosures on the same day. percentage drop equal to 20%. In terms of absolute count of removed credentials, Stripe credentials are removed the most (145 keys), followed by AWS and OpenAI—each with 109 keys each removed by Day 14. On an average, 47% credentials associated with each service were removed. On the other hand, credential revocations are less common than the removals with—verified credential exposures dropping from 1,175 to 865—a drop of only about 26% within 14 days of the disclosure. It is important to note that we observe Day 0 totals for removals and revocations to be different, likely because our IP address was blocked by services— Alibaba, RazorPay, and Twilio during credential verification crawl. AWS credentials have the highest revocations (122 revocations or 55%) while Telegram and Bitly credentials are the two least revoked ones, with only 1 revocation each. There could be several explanations behind aggregated removals (50%) to be approximately 2 times the aggregated revocations (26%)—(1) websites believe credential removals to be sufficient to prevent any further abuse; (2) credentials were no longer in use or were public or read-only; (3) website uses the credentials for more than one purposes, making it cost/resource prohibitive to revoke. 

Effect of Disclosures Across Websites. Next, we analyze the effect of our responsible disclosure across websites. Figure 6 represents the aggregated impact of removals and revocations across services, while Figure 7 shows, per service, the fraction of websites that remove, revoke, both, or neither. Services like GitHub, OpenAI, Mailchimp, Slack, and Telegram observed a larger fraction of credentials being removed but not revoked, suggesting developers often relied on partial fixes that failed to eliminate backend risks. Figure 11 in Appendix represents a CDF of remediations across different days post disclosure for all API services. Credential removals appear to greatly vary across different services—where close to 90% websites using OpenAI demon-strate atleast 1 credential removal while only 50% Bitly- and 15% SendGrid-using websites to perform removal of the exposed credentials—suggesting a widespread divergence in how providers react to security practices depending on the involved service. Except Bitly, we observe an upwards trend in temporal credential removals, showing that our disclosures overall impact. RazorPay 

> Alibaba
> Twilio
> Cloudflare
> Stripe
> AWS

> % Webpages with Exposed

> Revoked NOT Removed

Figure 7: Distribution of % websites engaging in removal AND revocation, ONLY removal, ONLY revocations, or none by service name. 

Geographic Effect of Disclosure Remediations. To assess geographical variation in remediation, we test, by country, the fraction of websites that remove and revoke credentials. Figure 12 in Appendix shows that websites in Italy demon-strate the highest revocations, followed up India and Japan. In terms of removals, around more than 60% of websites based in South Korea appear to have removed their exposed credentials the most, followed by Brazil, Russia, and Taiwan. In fact, Russia, Ukraine, and Taiwan are amongst the bottom three with the least percentage of the revocation. 

Category and Rank of Websites. Now, we test how reme-diations vary based on the categorization and popularity of websites. To this end, we compare binary removal and revo-cation outcomes across Tranco rank categories and website categories as demonstrated in Figure 10 in Appendix. We use pairwise two-proportion z-tests to statistically compare different rank categories and top-5 site categories amongst themselves. Cohen’s h (i.e., an arcsine-transformed effect size) quantifies the magnitude of differences. Figure 10 in Appendix (a) (left) clearly shows that credential removals are significantly (p <0.01) more common amongst popular websites (1K-500K) when compared against 500K-1M as well as 1M+ category. Interestingly, for credential revocations (Figure 10 in Appendix (a) (right)), we notice a unique trend wherein very popular websites (i.e., ranked upto top-50K) as well as less popular websites (i.e., ranked 500K+) demonstrate statistically significantly less credential revocations than the websites with average popularity (i.e., ranked 50K-500K). This might suggest that very popular websites may have more complex workflows integrating the same exposed credentials, making credential revocation difficult; while less popular sites may lack the security maturity or operational capacity to systematically remove or revoke exposed credentials. Figure 10 in Appendix (b) shows that Computers- and Education- and B2B-related websites can be seen to remove credentials statistically significantly more than Business, Ecommerce, or Computers verticals. While websites in Ecom-merce vertical tends to engage in removals or revocations statistically significantly (p <0.01) less than other verticals. Taken together, these differences indicate that technically oriented verticals exhibit better remediation practices. 

6.2. Root Cause Analysis of Credential Exposure 

Out of 70 responses (i.e., non-automated), 53 recipients acknowledged our disclosures—39 of which were unaware about the exposures. Through our disclosures, we asked for feedback to understand the cause of the reported exposure. A total of 40 recipients provided insights about the root cause, which we manually analyze by assigning one or more causal categories. Figure 8 depicts the distribution of 14 distinct root cause categories that emerge from these responses. One of the most common causes reported by 8 recipients was JavaScript-based bundling issue. This correlates with our findings in Section 5.3 that a significant part of the credentials are found in JavaScript bundles. Providers re-ported, as part of this bundling process, environment variables (e.g., process.env. APIKEY in Anecdote 1) are replaced with actual values from environment settings, resulting in referenced private credential values being embedded into public bundles that are deployed on website’s server and rendered by user’s browser when they visit the website. 

Anecdote 1. “The exposed value seems to be built into the public building assets despite being set to private in the nuxtjs framework. We use the nuxtjs (vue) framework. In a few places, credentials were referred to directly from process.env, rather than the $config parameter. Coupled with the import of an npm package @nuxtjs/dotenv, this 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

> Number of Websites
> JS bundling error
> Developer-made coding error
> Oversight
> Prefix-matched ENV variable inclusion in build
> Safe-to-expose public key
> Safe-to-expose read-only key
> Exposure from third-party error
> Error made by clients integrating the service
> Public vs private key referencing issue
> Credentials added at test-time persisted
> API key masking filter missing
> Exposure from CI/CD pipeline issue
> Configuration error
> Exposure from data modeling design choice
> Causes of Credential Exposure

Figure 8: Causes of Credential Exposure on the Web as identified from responses to our disclosure emails. 

caused all env variables to be baked in to the build even though they were never used in client side code. We are resolving.” 

Among responses that highlighted the bundling issue (Anec-dote 4), we coded an additional cause category, namely “prefix-matched ENV variable inclusion in build”, that ex-plains the inherent convention that industry follows to prevent exposing private variables into client-side bundles. This allows developers to use variable-naming convention prefixes such as REACT_APP_ for React and NEXT_PUBLIC_ 

or NUXT_PUBLIC_ for Next.js/Nuxt.js for variable that they want to be included in browser-facing code. However, these conventions can either be overridden by certain plugins (e.g., @nuxtjs/dotenv described in previous anecdote) or some configuration issue can cause all environment variable prefixes to be considered for inclusion within the bundle, resulting in exposure of even private variables and credentials. Another related causal category that was noticed in the responses was “public vs. private key referencing issue” where incorrect key variable referencing error resulted in the exposure (Anecdote 2). 

Anecdote 2. “The exposed key was an API token specif-ically intended for use with a certain Cloudflare feature that should only be accessed by our internal backend system. Unfortunately, we were mistakenly referencing the variable as ‘PUBLIC_CLOUDFLARE_TOKEN’ in-stead of the non-public ‘CLOUDFLARE_TOKEN’. The inclusion of the “PUBLIC” prefix caused the token to be compiled into a publicly accessible script, leading to the exposure.” 

“Developer-made coding errors” were observed to be the second most commonly attributed cause. Sometimes a developer could be testing a new feature or an integration (Anecdote 5) on server-side requiring private credentials and may forget to remove them once the testing concludes (“credentials added at test-time persisted”). Alternatively, it could have been an unintentional error made by the developer that could have resulted in accidental leakage of the credentials. Overall, ∼30% of cases (13) suggested that the leaked keys were actually “in-use” on the client-side, while the rest ∼70% of responses (31) described the leakage to be “accidental”. Several recipients (Anecdote 7) described “oversight” to be one of the reasons as well. For instance, some businesses did not remove the legacy references from their codebase even after removing the service, causing an unintended exposure from oversight which stems from several reasons—such as limitation on available resources, limited budgets, fast-paced business culture, pressure to deliver, inexperienced or relative new developers on the development team, coding error, and lack of or ignorance towards security best practices in lieu of speed or demonstration of value. Some businesses justify the reported credential exposure to be safe under two categories, i.e., “safe-to-expose public key” (5) and “safe-to-expose read-only key” (4), mainly in relation to AWS and GitHub credentials. The presented arguments described usage of keys—keys to have a read-only scope to the blog’s content, tokens to have a public-only scope: “read:discussion”, and keys to have restrictive IAM policy for read-only or public data ingestion purposes—to jus-tify the exposure to be non-problematic. However, follow-up clarification revealed that several recipients were unaware that these credentials in fact granted broader, unintended permis-sions. Our further investigation showed that even ostensibly “safe” public or read-only credentials (e.g., for data ingestion) can, once leaked, enable adversaries to flood backend services, inflate operational costs, poison downstream data, and create latent privilege-escalation risk if their scope is later extended. Finally, we hypothesize that some organizations, concerned about reputational and compliance risks, sought to frame the reported exposure as safe by design rather than as a lapse in security controls or vendor oversight, even though our disclosures were frequently followed by prompt removal or revocation of the reported keys. Overall, many responses highlight that some companies—potentially the small ones— prioritize efficiency, speed, cost, and resources over security. On the other hand, a major hosting platform attributed (Anecdote 9) several exposures to client-side configuration errors, noting that customers had manually entered Stripe credentials into fields intended for publishable keys, and framed the incident as incorrect user input interacting with their backend. Other responses describe issues such as— configuration issue with third-party Google Tag Manager (“configuration error”), credentials being defined within a JSON/GraphQL object that was dumped as it is in the payload (“Exposure from data modeling design choice”), lack of a filter that masks API key (“API key masking filter missing”), and unintentional exposure from a commit that occurred as part of the CI/CD pipeline (“exposure from CI/CD pipeline issue”). Around 14 responses indicated (e.g., Anecdote 3) that the recipients were aware about the exposure. There were various causes behind such intentional exposure as discussed previously such as developmental testing or misconception about all public keys to be safe to expose. One of the responses revealed that the developer not only intentionally exposed the key, but also made efforts to hide the exposure to evade internet scanning tools. 

Anecdote 3. “We were indeed aware of this particular issue, though it regrettably remained unaddressed for a period.” 

7. Limitations 

In our study, we analyzed 14 service types, as discussed in Section 4. Websites may contain additional credential types that fall outside the scope of this study. Furthermore, our detection relies on TruffleHog for identifying credentials, which may not capture all instances—for example, credentials stored in encoded or encrypted formats that the tool does not support. Despite extensive efforts, our responsible disclosure process may not have reached all affected organizations. In addition, we limited disclosures to verified credentials only, as both our results in Section 5 and prior work [6] highlight the inherent false-positive rates in pattern-based credential detection, and we aimed to avoid issuing false-positive notifications to organizations. 

8. Recommendations 

Based on our empirical measurements and disclosure feedback, we provide the following practical recommenda-tions for stakeholders in the web-ecosystem. 

For Developers. Our results show that most exposed cre-dentials originate from first-party JavaScript resources and build artifacts. We therefore recommend: (1) adopting strict 

environment-variable allowlisting, where only variables ex-plicitly prefixed for public use are eligible for inclusion in bundles; (2) avoiding client-side credentials by routing sensitive API calls through backend proxies; (3) integrating credential-scanning into CI/CD for both source and generated bundles, failing builds when high-confidence credentials are detected; (4) enforcing static checks (e.g., pre-commit hooks) that reject common credential patterns and direct references to sensitive environment variables; and (5) using separate, easily revocable test credentials in non-production environments to limit the impact of accidental exposures. 

For Framework and Tool Maintainers. Credential exposure in our dataset is frequently linked to bundling behavior and framework-level handling of environment variables. Frame-works and build tools can reduce this risk by: (1) shipping secure-by-default configuration systems where developers can explicitly declare which configuration entries are client-visible, treating all undeclared entries as secret and failing the build or raising a prominent warning if they are referenced in client code; (2) providing build-stage exposure detection that scans bundles for high-entropy tokens and known credential formats before deployment; and (3) clearly documenting and exemplifying the separation between server-only and client-visible configuration, avoiding recommended patterns that place long-lived credentials in client code. 

For Service Providers. Exposed credentials in our study frequently involve cloud, payment, and messaging APIs, including cases where operators incorrectly treated “pub-lic” credentials as safe. Service providers can reduce this risk by: (1) offering clearly separated key types for client and server use, where client-side credentials are narrowly scoped, rate-limited, origin-bound, and revocable without disrupting backend workflows; (2) adopting default-deny permission models, in which keys start with minimal scopes and require explicit elevation for sensitive operations; (3) deploying credential-scanning techniques to detect keys on public webpages, notify affected customers, and automatically throttle, rotate, or suspend obviously abused credentials; and (4) providing integration guidance and defaults that avoid long-lived static credentials in client code, instead promoting short-lived credentials, per-environment separation, and automated rotation. 

9. Conclusion 

By analyzing exposed credentials across 14 service types, this study identifies weak credential management as a systemic risk on the modern web. Our findings align with the recently published OWASP Top 10 (2025) [43], reaffirming that “Security Misconfiguration” remains one of the most significant contributors to web insecurity. We further show that the risks associated with exposed credentials on the web extend beyond online environments, enabling potential supply-chain compromise in real-world systems. Our technical analysis and large-scale disclosure effort reveal the long-term persistence of exposed credentials, which often remain publicly accessible for years. Crucially, we characterize the web-specific nature of these exposures, demonstrating that dynamic analysis is vital for identifying them. Our results show that the vast majority of leaks are introduced during the build process and materialize exclusively in live production environments (e.g., within JavaScript bundles), making the static scanning methods used in prior work fundamentally insufficient for the web. The collected feedback and remediation data provide insight into the root causes of these exposures, ranging from de-velopment and deployment practices to build-tool behavior. Our responsible disclosure campaign led to a measurable reduction in credential exposure across affected services, demonstrating that coordinated reporting can materially improve web security. Grounded in this empirical study and the disclosure feedback, we derive recommendations for website developers, framework and tool maintainers, and service providers. We find that the current state of credential management on the web is concerning and exposes services to systemic risk. Addressing this problem requires coordinated action from website operators, software vendors, and service providers to reduce the likelihood and impact of credential misuse. We hope that our findings contribute to increased awareness and foster further discussion within the community. 

10. Ethics considerations 

Our Institutional Review Board (IRB) has determined that this research does not involve human subjects as defined in 45 CFR 46.102(e) or 21 CFR 50.3(g). In this study, we handle sensitive data (exposed credentials in public resources). In all phases of the study, we ensure that all data are securely stored and accessed only by authorized personnel, maintaining 

restricted physical and digital access to any study data. In the scanning phase, we run our measurements from a highly secured, dedicated environment and store all data within it. To validate the credentials, we ensured that our methodology interacts only with non-intrusive endpoints and does not store any logs, data, responses, or other information from these endpoints. We only use the endpoints to verify whether credentials are valid (i.e., based on status codes). In the disclosure phase, we responsibly disclosed the issue to affected parties. For security reasons, we only included a prefix of each identified credential in our notifications. We also sent reminders and provided suggestions to affected organizations, which led to a significant reduction in overall risk on the web and was repeatedly acknowledged positively by the contacted organizations. While, similar to prior work, our disclosure methodology could not notify all affected organizations, we manually identified and prioritized those in critical infrastructure sectors (e.g., healthcare, government) and notified them responsibly. While our study shows that the majority of exposed credentials were removed, we acknowledge that it is not possible to eliminate all potential harm. Adversaries could theoretically replicate our methodology, as with many other vulnerability research studies, and cause damage. Therefore, we also contacted major service providers (e.g., Microsoft, Amazon Web Services) to take proactive measures toward sustainably addressing this issue.
