Appendix D. Monitoring Crawler Details 

We developed a custom browser crawler to visit webpages and collect network traffic data (i.e., requests, responses, headers, and payload) on a daily basis to monitor credential Yash Vekaria <yvekaria@ucdavis.edu> 

Credential Exposure Detected on {webpage}                              

> 1 message
> Yash Vekaria <yvekaria.09@gmail.com> Mon, Nov 10, 2025 at 12:15 AM
> To: yvekaria@ucdavis.edu
> Dear Team,
> We are security researchers from XXXXXXX University . We're reaching out about potential security problem(s) in
> resources hosted on web pages such as XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX that are embedded by different websites. We
> have detected that these resources have exposed credentials related to Stripe and AWS .
> Webpages with credential exposure: XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX ;XXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
> Proof of credential exposure:
> - Service: Stripe
> Credentials: [ sk_****_***** ***]
> The leak was observed w.r.t the following request(s) (website): XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
> XXXXXXXXXXXXXXX (on website: XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX )
> - Service: AWS
> Credentials: [ ************** ***]
> The leak was observed w.r.t the following request(s) (website): XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
> XXXXXXXXXXXXXXX (on website: XXXXXXXXXXXXXXX )
> We recommend taking the following steps to fully protect from this exposure: (1) remove credentials from all
> resources where they were exposed (2) revoke the exposed credentials.
> We kindly request your feedback regarding this notification on what caused the reported credential
> exposure(s) and whether you were already aware about it . This work is part of an ongoing security research
> project at {XXXXXXX University }and your input will be very valuable in helping improve web security practices. Your
> response will be treated confidentially and analyzed only in anonymized form. Should you have any questions or
> need further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact us.
> Best wishes,
> XXXXXXXXXXX
> XXXXXXX University

Figure 9: Email Template of our Responsible Disclosures removals. The crawler was implemented using Puppeteer (v24.24.1) for Chrome (v142) on NodeJS (v18.19.0), fol-lowing best practices of web crawling. We hook onto Puppeteer’s page.on() to persistently capture network traffic in headful state by remote tunneling the display (using 

tigervnc-standalone-server and x11vnc ). Each website was visited independently in a stateless manner (with 60s load timeout) using a separate user-data directory and stealth flags (e.g. overriding window.navigator object) to avoid bot detection. We additionally wait 15s once the page is fully loaded, to allow capturing any pending network traffic related to webpage resources. In order to crawl thousands of webpages on a daily basis, we run 20 instances of parallel docker containers (Docker v28.5.1) to quickly collect data. 

Appendix E. Anecdotes 

To complement our quantitative analysis, we include representative anecdotes (Anectode 4– 9) from disclosure responses to illustrate the diversity of real-world causes behind credential exposures. These representative anecdotes reflect a range of issues – from build pipeline oversights and legacy design choices to intentional but risky client-side implementations. 

Anecdote 4. “The issue arose from relying on the convention that only environment variables with a specific prefix should be exposed in the build, as unused variables should be automatically excluded.” 

Anecdote 5. “An ex-developer made a change to expose all the metadata (that included credentials) through the deployment, either by accident or for testing purposes. The security flaw carried over as the digital platform was built using a copy of Epona.” 

Anecdote 6. “I’m very curious about your detection methodology. We implemented very basic obfuscation methods primarily to prevent basic static analysis tools from flagging them and generating noise. Specifically, on older code we still use a simple Base64 encoding (in ***) blob at the top), while on newer code we use a string “scramble” (in ***). Which one obfuscation did you detect? The base64 one, the “scramble” one or both?” 

Anecdote 7. “The exposed token was due to human error and not a bug, and unfortunately the company didn’t have checks in place specifically to catch it - it’s not something they thought would’ve been leaked.” 

Anecdote 8. “We specifically use the Firehose service, which doesn’t directly allow public ingestion via unauthen-ticated endpoints. We therefore chose to use a dedicated credential with a highly-restricted IAM policy that only allows data insertion (firehose: PutRecord) to a specific data stream. So, to answer your questions: • No, we did not remove nor revoke the credentials, as they are intended to be public. • They do not pose a security risk as their permissions are strictly limited to their intended purpose (client-side data ingestion) and have no access to any other AWS resource. • The only risk is a malicious actor submitting junk data or attempting to drive up our costs through excessive submissions. This is an inherent risk in any public data ingestion system, and we have monitoring and mitigation plans, such as credential rotation, in case of abuse. We are aware that using AWS credentials client-side isn’t ideal but we chose to do so mostly as a cost-saving solution: maintaining a proxy server solely to sign the PutRecord API request from a client-provided record would introduce non-negligible costs without providing a meaningful security benefit in this specific context.” 

Anecdote 9. “The exposure appears to be caused by users/client manually entering their Stripe secret keys (sk_...) into a field meant for publishable keys (pk_...). These keys are user-provided and not generated or stored by our systems, which indicates that the issue stems from incorrect manual input rather than a system-side data leak or misconfiguration. We already introduced frontend validation in the past to prevent this type of mistake, so the affected entries were likely made before that change was implemented.” Top 1K
